Okay, I'm going to tell you guys my story. When I was a little boy, grownups used to come up to me and say, what do you want to be when you grow up? And I would say a direct response copywriter. Obviously, that's not true. I stumbled into this business just like everybody else here. I was a struggling real estate broker at the time. And this was around 1990 and the bottom of the market had fallen out, just like what happened recently. People were upside down on their homes. Nobody could sell. Stuff wasn't selling. Brokers were dropping out of the business. And I was living in a one bedroom apartment, no furniture. Four months behind my rent. And the way I would eat is I'd take the credit card, use it to buy groceries. And then a month later, the bill would come, take a second credit card, use that to pay the minimum payment on the first card, use a third credit card to pay the minimum payment on the second card and just see how long I could keep this house of cards going while I'm hoping for that next big sales commission so I can do something about it. And the company I was working for was Herding, too. They were losing like tens of thousands of dollars a month and the sales manager had just quit. His last official act as sales manager was to put his fist through the window in a drunken rampage, put his fist through the window of his 16th floor office and storm out. And there was this little trail of blood from his office through the bullpen area, out through the front of the reception area, out the door into the elevator. So I'm on this sinking ship, right? And I get this brilliant idea. I should be the next sales manager. So I go up to the owner of the company and I say, hey, I was like in my 20s, right? Most of the people in the office had been selling real estate when I was still in diapers and I'm thinking like I'm going to be the sales manager here. So I go to the guy who owned the company and I say, hey, I've got this plan, how to turn this company around. And no salary. You could just pay me override on the commissions of the other agents. And plus, I'll get to work with my own clients. And what was I doing there? I was making a risk-free offer. So the guy said, sure, why not? So I get this gig as the sales manager and I'm like, okay, the first thing I need to do, I need to learn marketing and advertising because we want to attract better agents to work for us. We want to attract better listings. And so lo and behold, as if on cue, I get this promotion in the mail for something called the Gary Halbert letter. And so I'm reading this promotion and there's all these like amazing bullets of what you're going to discover if you become a subscriber. And I was sold. I sent in my $195 on the credit card. And when you subscribe, you get this free report, how to make maximum money a minimum time. So it had all these cool things. But one chapter was on how to sell information products and make money doing that. I'm like, that's it. I'm going to write a book on how to sell your home in a declining market. Because I knew how to do that. It wasn't so good at getting listings. But once I got them, I knew how to sell them. So first thing I did is I sat down and I wrote a sales letter. And I made every outrageous promise I could think of in this sales piece. And then after that, I said, okay, now what do I have to write in this book to pay off these promises to make the sales letter 100% true? And so that's what I did. So I wrote this book. And then I took out my credit card again, financed the mailing, put the suckers out in the mail, and waited for the money to come pouring in. And what do you think happened? Nothing. Not a single order. So I'm like, oh, crap. So I called Gary Halbert's office and tell him, and they go, okay, you've got to call this copywriter in California named Brad Peterson. Back at the time, Brad was doing these quickie $200 critiques. So I take a cash advance. I pay him the $200. He looks at my copy. He says, you know what? Actually, this is pretty good. He says, your bullets especially are pretty good, but your headline on your opening are all wrong. See what I was doing is I had gotten all these things from a Gora mail, all this gloom and doom stuff. So I just modeled that, right? Oh, this is how you write copies. So it was all like, why the real estate market's going to crash even further? And it had all these macroeconomic reasons, and you better sell now or you're screwed. And Brad goes, they already know they want to sell. Just tell them how to sell it. He says, see these first two pages? Cut them out. Start here. Okay. So it was a good lesson, and it's a very important lesson, because even today, like the biggest mistake I see with new copywriters is they take a successful formula and they misapply it. When you swipe, it has to be appropriate to what you're doing, right? So got that lesson from Brad. So I took his advice. I put the sucker in the mail again, spent another whatever it was to do another mailing on the credit card. And this time, I waited a week later, I opened the mailbox, and there's envelopes in it with checks from total strangers. And I'm hooked, right? It's like, I'm a heroin addict now. So I got a 3% response on that mailing, right? Before you clapped too much. I knew nothing about the business side. So I'm spending too much money on printing, too much money on postage, too much money on the books. Price point was too low. I had no back end product. I thought like it was like, you know, Joe Carbo, Lazy Man's Way to Riches, you sell one book and then you throw the names away because you're going to make millions of dollars. So I'm doing a 3% response and I'm losing my shirt because I don't know what the hell I'm doing. I also didn't know that 3% was a good response. So not knowing that, I figure, well, I guess the solution is to get a higher response. So I go back and I keep perfecting my craft and I keep tweaking the letter and mailing it. Got it up to 6%. And at 6% I was finally making money, but I wasn't making enough money to live on and I certainly wasn't making enough money to get myself out of the hole I had dug for myself. So I decided, all right, got to do more products. So I had heard Gary Halbert say that one of the perennial things that's always selling is the flat abs stuff, right? So there was this guy who I worked with in the real estate office. He was 50 years old and he was ripped. I mean, he had, you know, they didn't have the term six back back then. We used to call it washboard abs, but whatever it was, he had them, right? And I said, hey, how do you do this? And he told me, I said, let's create a product. I'll do all the work. You put up the money. We'll be 50-50 partners. He goes, okay. So did the whole thing, wrote the sales letter, created the product, hired a friend of mine who was an artist to do the drawings of the exercises, put it out there. And I named the company Instant Health and Fitness Inc. And I got the idea from Gene Schwartz because he had a company called Instant Improvement. And a matter of fact, first list I ever rented was Gene Schwartz's list. And we mail it out and it makes a profit. So then this is great. Let's mail more names. We mail those out and that made a profit too. So now, like, if I had been smart, I would have continued doing what was working. But instead, I went to a seminar and there was a salesman there and he was selling card decks. You guys know what card decks are? Card decks, it's like Valpak, except instead of selling lawn care restaurants, they have a different card each selling a different product. And it's all wrapped in cellophane and they mail out mostly it's B2B. The reason it was enticing to me was it was costing me like $50 per thousand names to rent Gene Schwartz's mailing list. And the card deck, you could get a thousand names, I don't know, for like $10. And it included printing and postage. So my greed got the better of me. My common sense went out the window. I said, let's do it. And the problem is that they had a minimum order, which was like, I don't know, $10,000, which was all the money, including all the profits we had had from the previous mailings. So I do this thing. I got like two orders. So no, back to zero and I'm apologizing profusely to my co-worker and he looks at me and he says, hey, it's all right, man. Nobody put it down to my head. We tried it, we did it, it's cool. So that was great. So anyway, by now it's like three years later and I've been getting beaten up by the School of Hard Knocks. And I was doing real estate during the day. I was doing all the copywriting mail order stuff at night and weekends. And the market was looking up and plus I had started incorporating some of the stuff I learned into my real estate, doing mailings to start and it was working. But I actually, I despised it. I had grown to despise real estate sales. I hated getting up in the morning. And I said, okay, once this copywriting stuff takes off, I'm going to quit real estate. And then just finally came to the realization that I'm spreading myself too thin. If I want to make the mail order, the copywriting, direct response stuff work, I just got to quit real estate altogether and dive into it full time. And so that's what I did. Had no idea where, how I was going to make money, no idea where I was going to make it, get clients. I had no contacts in the industry. But I said, like Richard Branson, screw it, let's do it. So there was an event coming up in Las Vegas and a lot of the big names were going to be speaking there. And so, took out the credit card. But, you know, by the way, when people say, how'd you learn how to write copy? I'm messing out my credit cards. That's how I learned how to write copy. So I went to this event and I get there and the place is packed, packed, packed, packed. I'm looking around saying there's got to be 500 people here. So that night, I went to Kinkos, to the local Kinkos, and I wrote a sales letter selling my services as a copywriter. And I made 500 copies the next morning. I stood outside the place handing them out to people. Now you heard about Guillermo and Paul talk about how they're introverts. How many of you would classify yourselves as introverts? You could tell the introverts because they go like this. So I am too, okay? And I was standing outside there handing these things out. I felt like I'm handing out massage parlor leaflets on 8th Avenue. I was so nervous I thought I was going to throw up. And it was talk about fear and going outside your comfort zone. I'm thinking here I am making an ass of myself in front of this industry that I'm trying to be a part of. But you know, got to do what you got to do, right? So I gave them out and I ended up getting five gigs out of it. So would you guys like to see the sales letter? The other day we have high tech things. I forgot what that means. 15? Ah crap. All right. Let's go quickly through this. How to hire a world class copywriter for Pocket Chains was the headline. I just wanted to get gigs. I didn't care how cheaply. I wanted to have samples to show other people because you got to start somewhere, right? If you'd like to see sales of your product or service go through the roof but you can't afford to spend 10,000 to 15,000 for a copywriter, here's good news. So I get like embarrassed when I read this because it's like I look at it and I go, ooh, did I really write that? And if you're doing well, you will cringe with embarrassment when you look at your old stuff. It's a sign that you're growing. It's a good sign. So the big mistake here is says, but you can't afford to spend it. What am I going after? People who can't afford it, right? Big mistake. But anyway, if you look at it, it has a classic Gary Halbert if then opening and then it gets to why because I've created more than my share of winners for myself and others and I can do the same for you. And it says among the pieces I've written and here's the key. These are the bullet points of what you've done and that's going to be the heart of your sales pitch. They want to know before I spend money hiring this guy or gal, what have they done? So you tell them what you've done. Now when you're new, you haven't done a whole heck of a lot, right? And you've got to take what you've got and sell the hell out of it. So here I am. I have these bullets and all of them are 100% true, but I'm gilding the lily. It's like when boardroom says, what never to eat on the airplane? And the answer is nothing. Don't eat food on an airplane. Or when, you know, Agora has the Jaffy royalty secret and you find out it's dividend stocks. But if you said dividend stocks, nobody but I, right? So here it says the first bullet, a six page letter that pulled 6% cash with order, which is true that I didn't have a credit card merchant account, from a compiled list. And compiled list, do you guys know what a compiled list is? Anybody not know? All right. So compiled lists suck. A compiled list means you raised your hand. Basically, it's not a list of people who've ever bought anything. It's just a list that was compiled. And in this case, it was a list of people selling their homes, which is a pretty damn good list if you're going to sell a thing on how to sell a home. But it was a compiled list. The second bullet, a one page lead generator, again, mailed to a compiled list and a follow-up that won in $23 in revenue for every $1. Is that freaking amazing? 23 to 1? Well, here's the thing. This was a letter that I sent out to homeowners asking them if they would want to sell their house. One percent of them would respond. We'd list it. I'd make a $30,000 sales commissioner, whatever the hell it was. Do the math, 23 to 1. Here's the third one, an ad that helped sell over 5,000 copies of a book on Manhattan parking garages. If I can make parking sound interesting, imagine what I could do with your product or service. So the deal there was there was this woman and she noticed that if you want to go to Manhattan, like Carnegie Hall or Lincoln Center or something, and you park, one garage would be charging you like $15 and the other one would be charging you $8. She just was like, screw it. I'm going to make a directory. And so you can shop. So before you go into the city, you know where to park. And she thought she was going to be like the next Zagats, you know, what to do with restaurants. She had it the same size. And she, anyway, it was a disaster. Most of these sales we sold as bulk that we got people to give away to their clients and stuff, but I helped her out with that for free. And so I got to, you know, put it on my list of accomplishments. So you look at the bottom and it says, as a result of these successes, I recently decided to quit my day job as a realtor parentheses for the past 10 years. I've been selling high priced luxury properties in New York City, New York. That has a lot of cachet, right? Oh, New York City and go into copywriting full time. And I'd love the chance to use my copywriting skills to help you sell more of your product or service than you ever dreamed. Until now, most of my work has been on my own projects, which means that very few people have heard of me yet, right? Some Y explanation, right? And very important word yet. As a result, you can still hire me for only a small fraction of what I'll be charging in the not too distant future. Right? So that's urgency, quote, right? Sense of urgency. Sound interesting. They come talk to me. I'll be here for the rest of, and they had a call to action. Okay. So I got five, five gigs out of this. Two of them were wannabes who never mailed the damn thing I wrote for them. Two of them were legitimate, small time businesses, but you know, they worked for them. And one of them was a guy named Chip Wood who had a company called SoundView Publications. The short story, of course, is I'm 20 years later, I'm still working with them. The long story is they don't hire you back right away. They want to see how it does. And in that case, they discontinued the product. So there were a lot of trials and tribulations along the way. But I think this should give you a good idea of how some total doofus with no experience, no contacts, no money gets started and gets some clients. So I hope you found it valuable. Thank you.
